coredata.dc:title: A refractory wound healing hydrogel with integrated functions of photothermal anti-infection, superoxide dismutase mimicking activity, and intelligent infection management
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Chuan
Wang, Yu
Liu, Hui
Wu, Ye
Wang, Yi
Cao, Zhenxing
Li, Weilong
Peng, Yan
Xiong, Hui
Jin, Biqiang
Kong, Qingquan
Wu, Jinrong
coredata.dc:description: Promoting refractory wound healing remains a challenging issue. Currently, dressings with intelligent infection management functions built on infection eradication and reactive oxygen species (ROS) scavenging abilities are lacking. In this study, an approach based on Cu2+-imidazole coordination cross-linked histidine grafted chitosan (CS-His) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was developed to engineer superoxide dismutase (SOD) mimicking centers with photothermal anti-infection and intelligent anti-infection abilities, beyond typical ROS scavenging activity. The cross-linked coordination network was further promoted with tanshinol-encapsulated ZIF-8 frameworks (ZIF-8@Ts) to achieve intelligent infection management behaviors. These designs generate a refractory wound-healing hydrogel with integrated functions of photothermal anti-infection, SOD mimicking activity, and intelligent infection management. In response to infection exacerbation, the SOD mimicking centers and ZIF-8@Ts nanoparticles could release CQDs for anti-infection and Ts for antioxidation, respectively. In vitro results revealed photothermal anti-infection, ROS scavenging, and intelligent infection management capacities of the hydrogel. These abilities promoted the M2 transformation of macrophages and rescued the inhibitory effect of infection on fibroblast proliferation and migration. The hydrogel exhibited high regenerative efficacy in a rat model of chronically infected diabetic wounds. These results demonstrated the immense potential of the intelligent hydrogel integrated with multifunctions for refractory wound healing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Refractory wound
Superoxide dismutase
Photothermal anti-infection
Intelligent infection management
M2 transformation
Reactive oxygen species

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro and in vivo hemostasis
In vitro biocompatibility, ROS scavenging, and macrophage polarization assay
Preparation and characterization of NPs, polymers, and hydrogel

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Conception and design of the SOD-AO hydrogel. a) Structural schematics of the hydrogel. The hydrogel is capable of b) promoting hemostasis, c) eradicating infection via the photothermal effect of CQDs, d) scavenging ROS through the SOD mimicking geometry, and e) responsively releasing CQDs and Ts for intelligent infection management, thereby f) healing the refractory wound.

Characterizations of NPs. a) TEM image, b) XPS spectra, and c) XRD pattern of CQDs. d) TEM images, e) XRD pattern, and f–j) mapping of ZIF-8@Ts NPs. k) UV spectroscopy and l) FTIR spectra of ZIF-8@Ts NPs. m, n) Zetasizer tests of ZIF-8@Ts NPs under different conditions.

Characterization of polymers and hydrogel. 1H NMR spectra of a) CS-His and b) OHA. c) FTIR spectra of the hydrogel. d) SEM and mapping of SOD-AO hydrogel. e) Self-healing test of the SOD-AO hydrogel. f) The SOD-AO hydrogel was injected into the letters “SCU.” Rheological analysis including g) frequency, h) oscillatory strain sweep, and i) destruction and recovery of the control and SOD-AO hydrogel.

Photothermal effect, SOD-like activity, swelling property, degradation, and intelligent behaviors of the hydrogel. Photothermal effect of the hydrogel a, b) in vitro and c, d) in vivo. e) SOD mimicking activities of the hydrogel were determined using a commercial kit. f) Superoxide anion scavenging abilities determined with electron spin resonance (ESR) signals. g) Swelling properties of the hydrogel. h) Low pH-stimulated hydrogel degradation. Low pH-induced release of i) CQDs and j) Ts from the SOD-AO hydrogel. (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01).

Hemostatic properties of the hydrogel. a) BCI of the hydrogel. b) SEM images of the hemocyte adhesion on the hydrogel surface. c) In vivo hemostasis in a rat liver bleeding model. d) Quantitative analysis of blood loss, and e) hemostatic time. (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01).

In vitro antibacterial effect of the hydrogel. Survival rate of a) Staphylococcus aureus and b) Pseudomonas aeruginosa. c) Images of bacteria clones treated with different hydrogel and d) corresponding live/dead staining images. (**P < 0.01, SOD-AO hydrogel + IR: SOD-AO hydrogel combining PTT.).

In vitro biocompatibility and cell migration ability tests. a, b) Hemolytic analysis of the hydrogel. c) Live/dead staining of L929 cells treated with different hydrogels for 24 h and 48 h. d) CCK-8 analysis of L929 cells and e) lipopolysaccharide (LPS)-infected L929 cells treated with different hydrogel. f) Scratch assay of LPS-infected L929 cells treated with different hydrogel and corresponding g) quantitative analysis. The dashed lines indicate the scratch edge. (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01).

ROS scavenging ability and macrophage polarization assay. a) ROS scavenging effects in LPS stimulated RAW264.7 cells. b) Flow cytometry analysis of the phenotype of LPS-infected RAW264.7 cells treated with different hydrogel.

In vivo evaluation of the hydrogel in the treatment of chronically infected diabetic wound. a, b) Macroscopic evaluation of wound healing effects and corresponding quantitative results. c, d) In vivo anti-infection effect of the hydrogel. (**P < 0.01).

Histological evaluation of newborn tissue. a) H&E staining, b) Masson’s staining of the tissues. The black arrow indicates an abscess; the blue arrows, regenerated follicles; the brown arrow, a gland (brown scale bar = 20 µm); and the green arrow, a regenerated vessel (green scale bar = 5 µm). c, d) Immunohistochemical analysis of collagens I and III. Immunofluorescence staining of e) iNOS for M1, f) CD206 for M2, g) TNF-α for pro-inflammation, h) IL-10 for anti-inflammation, and i) angiogenic marker CD31. j) Thickness of the regenerated skin, k) collagen deposition, and l) density of neovascularization. (***P < 0.01, SOD-AO hydrogel + IR: SOD-AO hydrogel combining PTT group).

head.ce:title: A refractory wound healing hydrogel with integrated functions of photothermal anti-infection, superoxide dismutase mimicking activity, and intelligent infection management

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Orthopedic Research Institute, Department of Orthopedics, West China Hospital, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Orthopedic Research Institute, Department of Orthopedics, West China Hospital, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials Engineering,College of Polymer Science and Engineering, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, China

College of Polymer Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu 610066, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu 610066, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: C. G. and Y. W. contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Promoting refractory wound healing remains a challenging issue. Currently, dressings with intelligent infection management functions built on infection eradication and reactive oxygen species (ROS) scavenging abilities are lacking. In this study, an approach based on Cu2+-imidazole coordination cross-linked histidine grafted chitosan (CS-His) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was developed to engineer superoxide dismutase (SOD) mimicking centers with photothermal anti-infection and intelligent anti-infection abilities, beyond typical ROS scavenging activity. The cross-linked coordination network was further promoted with tanshinol-encapsulated ZIF-8 frameworks (ZIF-8@Ts) to achieve intelligent infection management behaviors. These designs generate a refractory wound-healing hydrogel with integrated functions of photothermal anti-infection, SOD mimicking activity, and intelligent infection management. In response to infection exacerbation, the SOD mimicking centers and ZIF-8@Ts nanoparticles could release CQDs for anti-infection and Ts for antioxidation, respectively. In vitro results revealed photothermal anti-infection, ROS scavenging, and intelligent infection management capacities of the hydrogel. These abilities promoted the M2 transformation of macrophages and rescued the inhibitory effect of infection on fibroblast proliferation and migration. The hydrogel exhibited high regenerative efficacy in a rat model of chronically infected diabetic wounds. These results demonstrated the immense potential of the intelligent hydrogel integrated with multifunctions for refractory wound healing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Refractory wounds can have severe complications, even disability and death.[1] The complex microenvironment characterized by severe infection, excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS), and impaired vascularization makes refractory wound healing challenging.[2,3] To address these problems, various wound dressings have been used.[4] Among these, microenvironment-responsive hydrogels with hemostatic, antimicrobial, ROS scavenging, and angiogenic activities exhibited high efficiency in treating refractory wounds.[3,5] However, few hydrogels have achieved comprehensively intelligent management of infection and related oxidative stress.

Superoxide dismutase (SOD), the most critical antioxidant that protects cells from excessive oxidative stress, is a principal metalloenzyme that catalyzes the decomposition of ROS. The SOD families consist of an active center with a transitional metal ion (Cu2+/Zn2+/Mn2+) coordinated by three histidine (His) residues via the imidazole group. Such active centers exhibit a high ROS scavenging ability.[6] Previously, Cu2+-, Co2+-, Fe2+-, Mn2+-, and Zn2+-centered metal coordination polymers mimicking the coordination geometry of natural SOD demonstrated considerable potential in protecting cells from high levels of ROS. [7–10] Moreover, the pH-sensitive coordination bonds within the SOD mimicking centers enable them to respond intelligently to infection exacerbation.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), exhibiting robust microbial killing efficiency and low drug resistance, is promising for anti-infection in refractory wounds.[11] Such antibacterial ability should be enhanced through its photothermal effects. [12,13] Moreover, owing to their modifiable surfaces, CQDs can be easily functionalized for further use.

In this study, we attempted to fabricate a hydrogel with not only SOD mimicking activity but also robust infection eradication and intelligent infection management behaviors, which was known as SOD-AO hydrogel. To this end, histidine-grafted chitosan (CS-His) and imidazole-functionalized CQDs were synthesized and coordinated with Cu2+ to construct SOD mimicking centers (Fig. 1
a). The photothermal effect of the CQDs endowed the SOD mimicking centers with photothermal anti-infection ability beyond the SOD-like activity. Moreover, the pH-sensitive property of the coordination bonds constructing the SOD mimicking centers could allow the pH-responsive release of antibacterial CQDs,[11] thereby intelligently killing microbes. For intelligent management of infection-generated ROS, the CS-His chains were dynamically cross-linked using highly biocompatible oxidized hyaluronic acid (OHA) through pH-sensitive Schiff base bonds.[14] At this point, a pH-sensitive metal–organic framework (ZIF-8) encapsulated with antioxidant tanshinol (Ts) was loaded onto the hydrogel. The pH responsiveness of the hydrogel network and ZIF-8@Ts could achieve infection-responsive release of Ts for intelligent ROS scavenging.[15] The dynamic network was injectable and self-healing, making the highly biocompatible hydrogel suitable for treating irregularly shaped wounds. Moreover, the prominent hemostatic ability of chitosan ensured the hydrogel to facilitate hemostasis in the wound, which was essential for healing.[16] After dressing the wound, the hydrogel initially stopped the bleeding (Fig. 1 b). The SOD mimicking centers subsequently enabled early eradication of infection through the photothermal anti-infection ability of CQDs (Fig. 1 c).[13] Early eradication of infection could possibly prevent the development of an acidic microenvironment, thereby providing a favorable microenvironment for the SOD mimicking geometry to catalyze ROS decomposition via the imidazole-coordinated Cu2+ (Fig. 1 d). The processes of infection eradication and ROS scavenging may promote the M2 transformation of macrophages, thereby facilitating refractory wound healing.[17] In case of infection exacerbation (Fig. 1 e), the generated acidic microenvironment could trigger the disintegration of the hydrogel network and SOD mimicking centers. Notably, this pH-responsive activity triggered the intelligent release of CQDs for anti-infection.[11] However, intelligently decomposed ZIF-8@Ts NPs release Ts for ROS scavenging. The intelligent management of infection prevents infection-induced break of M1–M2 macrophage polarization balance, enabling the hydrogel to expedite the wound healing process (Fig. 1 f).[17].

In the present study, the SOD mimicking centers with photothermal anti-infection ability beyond typical ROS scavenging activity were created in the dynamic hydrogel. The pH sensitivity of the coordination bonds in the SOD mimicking centers was utilized to achieve intelligent release of CQDs for on-demand anti-infection. Meanwhile, antioxidant Ts was first encapsulated in ZIF-8 before being loaded in the dynamic hydrogel network, thereby achieving intelligent management of infection-generated ROS. Such an intelligent hydrogel integrated with multifunctions exhibited a high potential to facilitate refractory wound healing.

Chitosan (CS, deacetylation degree > 95 %, MW 120–150 kDa), hyaluronic acid (HA, 2,000 kDa MW), histidine (His), citric acid (CA), zinc nitrate hexahydrate, and 2-methylimidazole were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Ts was acquired from Macklin (Shanghai, China).

The hydrothermal method was used to synthesize the imidazole-functionalized CQDs. The details are available in the Supplementary Material. For the synthesis of ZIF-8@Ts, 1 mL of 2-methylimidazole (1.4 M) containing 2 mg Ts solution was prepared. Then, 1 mL of zinc nitrate hexahydrate (20 mM) was added quickly, and the mixture was stirred for 30 min. The product was washed and lyophilized. Drug loading content and efficiency were revealed via ultraviolet spectrophotometry at 282 nm and calculated as described in the Supplementary Material.

For the synthesis of CS-His, N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) were used to catalyze the formation of amide bonds between His carboxyl groups and CS amino groups in morpholineethanesulfonic acid (0.5 M) buffer solution. OHA was prepared by oxidizing HA using NaIO4. The details for polymer synthesis are available in the Supplementary Material.

The morphology of NPs was characterized through scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Nova NanoSEM450) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi H-600, Japan). Zeta potential and size distribution of NPs were examined with the Zetasizer (Malvern Zetasizer Nano-ZS90 instrument, Malvern, UK). UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2401PC, Shimadzu) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS50 FTIR) were used to confirm the successful loading of Ts onto ZIF-8. X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) of NPs was performed with an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, ESCALAB 250Xi). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained with an X-ray diffractometer (Bruker D8 ADVANCE). The fluorescence spectrum of CQDs was tested with a fluorescence spectrometer (PicoQuant FT-300). FTIR and nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy (1H NMR) (Avance III HD 500 MHz, Bruker, Germany) were used to confirm the successful synthesis of OHA and CS-His.

The SOD-AO hydrogel was created by mixing the CS-His solution (5 %, w/v) containing CQDs (0.1 %, w/v) and ZIF-8@Ts (0.1 %, w/v) with an equal volume of OHA solution (10 %, w/v). CuCl2 was added to achieve a final concentration of 0.1 mM before modulating the pH to 8.5 for the formation of the dynamic Schiff base bonds between CS-His and OHA. The hydrogel was incubated at 37 °C for another 1 h to facilitate the formation of coordination between imidazole groups and Cu2+.

The self-healing, injectability, rheological, swelling, degradation, pH-responsive behaviors, and tissue adhesion of hydrogel were evaluated as detailed in the Supplementary Material. A thermographic camera (BritIR, Wuhan Guide Infrared Co., ltd.) was used to measure the temperature of hydrogel irradiated with an IR laser (780 nm, 100 mw, Zhongshan Shuanghong Electronics Co.). Then, the SOD mimicking activity was analyzed with a commercial SOD assay kit (Solarbio, Beijing, China). Xanthine and xanthine oxidase system was used to generate O2 –. The superoxide ion scavenging ability was further examined using electron spin resonance (ESR) (JEOL JES-FA200). CQD and Ts release activities were detected as detailed in the Supplementary Material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and in vivo hemostasis
ce:section.ce:para: Blood-clotting index (BCI) and blood cell adhesion assays were performed for in vitro evaluation of hemostatic capacities. A liver bleeding model of rat was used to test hydrogel hemostasis in vivo, as described in the Supplementary Material.

Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC25923) and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (ATCC27853) were co-cultured with hydrogel, and viability was examined with absorbance at 600 nm and through SYTO 9/propidium iodide (PI) staining.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro biocompatibility, ROS scavenging, and macrophage polarization assay
ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogel-mediated hemolysis was performed as described in the Supplementary Material. The proliferation and migration ability of L929 cells co-cultured with hydrogel were evaluated. LPS-stimulated murine macrophage RAW264.7 cells were used for ROS scavenging analysis. 2,7-dichlorofuorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA; APExBIO, USA) was used as a probe to detect intracellular ROS. The morphology of macrophages was tested with a flow cytometer (LSRFortessa, BD, USA).

The protocol was approved by the ethics committee of West China Hospital, Sichuan University (20220303009). We further evaluated the therapeutic efficacy of the SOD-AO hydrogel in vivo using a chronically infected diabetic wound model of a rat. Moreover, 60 µg/g body weight streptozotocin was used to create the diabetic rat model. Additional details are included in the Supplementary Material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of NPs, polymers, and hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para:
To fabricate SOD-mimicking centers with photothermal anti-infection ability, imidazole-functionalized CQDs were synthesized. TEM revealed the imidazole-functionalized CQDs with a uniform size of 4.0 ± 1.3 nm (Fig. 2
a, Fig. S1a). The peaks at 399 Ev, 400 Ev, 531 eV, and 532 eV corresponding to CC, CC, CN, CO, and CO on XPS spectra further showed the successful attachment of imidazole groups on the surface of CQDs (Fig. 2 b, Fig. S1b-d). In addition, this result was verified through the new emerging peaks at 1140 cm−1 that corresponded to the imidazole ring, whereas the broadband arising in the range of 3000–3300 cm−1 was attributable to the presence of NH2 in FTIR spectra (Fig. S1e). XRD pattern revealed a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 22.7°, thereby indicating the graphitic structure of the CQDs (Fig. 2 c), as described previously.[18] Furthermore, the zetapotential test revealed a net surface charge of − 17 ± 1.5 mV (Fig. S1f) on the CQDs. Such a negatively charged surface could possibly resist cell attachment (negatively charged surface) and consequently minimize the potential influence on cells.

For intelligent management of infection generated by ROS, Ts was loaded onto pH-sensitive ZIF-8. TEM and SEM illustrated the uniform size (200 ± 8.5 nm) of ZIF-8@Ts particles (Fig. 2 d, Figure S2), which was suitable for dispersion in the hydrogel. Crystal properties of ZIF-8@Ts were demonstrated by the XRD pattern (Fig. 2 e), showing peaks at 2θ = 7.4°, 10.4°, 12.8°, 14.7°, 16.5°, and 18.1°, matching those in previous studies.[19] ZIF-8@Ts and ZIF-8 NPs showed a similar morphology (Fig. 2 f–j, Fig. S2b–f). Successful loading of Ts onto ZIF-8 NPs was verified using a newly formed peak at 280 nm corresponding to Ts on UV spectroscopy (Fig. 2 k). The new peak at 1250 cm−1 corresponding to CO of catechol structure in FTIR spectra further indicated the successful loading of Ts on ZIF-8 NPs (Fig. 2 l). Encapsulation of Ts into ZIF-8 slightly decreased the positive charge density; however, it failed to significantly change the size of ZIF-8 NPs, which was revealed by Zetasizer tests (Fig. 2 m, n). After exposure to a pH = 5.0 environment, the unimodal of ZIF-8@Ts size distribution became multimodal, indicating the disintegration of the ZIF-8@Ts NPs (Fig. 2 m). This indicated that ZIF-8@Ts could possibly be disintegrated in the infectious environment, which was characterized by low pH.

CS-His was synthesized to construct a smart hydrogel with SOD-mimicking centers. New emerging peaks at 7.2 ppm, 8.2 ppm, and 7.79 ppm in 1H NMR spectra indicated the imidazole group of histidine and the formation of amido bond in CS-His molecule, respectively (Fig. 3
a). The grafting ratio was determined as 25.3 %. The successful synthesis of CS-His was also verified using FTIR spectroscopy, which showed new peaks at 1140 cm−1 that corresponded to NH vibration and new peaks at 833 cm−1 which corresponded to CN vibration in the imidazole ring (Fig. S3a).

To promote the pH responsiveness of the hydrogel, OHA was synthesized and used for cross-linking the hydrogel with Schiff base bonds. The new chemical shift at 4.82, 4.98, and 5.08 ppm in 1H NMR spectra indicated the presence of aldehydes in OHA (Fig. 3 b), and the degree of oxidation was 45.5 %. Moreover, the generation of aldehydes was exhibited by the new peak at 1730 cm−1 in FTIR spectra corresponding to the CO stretching of the aldehyde groups in OHA (Fig. S3b). The formation of Schiff base bonds in the hydrogel was illustrated by the peak covering 1590–1690 cm−1 on the FTIR spectra, corresponding to a blending peak of CN, CO stretching. Meanwhile, the peak at 1730 cm−1 on the FTIR spectra almost disappeared due to the consumption of aldehyde groups in OHA (Fig. 3 c). After Cu2+coordination, a new coordination peak appeared at 1068 cm−1 in addition to the peak at 1140 cm−1 corresponding to imidazole in the FTIR spectra (Fig. 3 c), indicating the successful coordination between imidazole groups and Cu2+. The fabrication strategy endowed the hydrogel with homogeneous structures, which were revealed through SEM (Fig. 3 d, Figure S4). Moreover, the dynamic cross-linking strategy provided the SOD-AO hydrogel with self-healing and injectable properties. As shown in Fig. 3 e, the integrity was restored in 10 s after the cut-off ends were allowed to reattach. The moderate conditions for self-healing made the hydrogel more suitable for medical use than other self-healing materials.[20] The injectability was illustrated by injecting the hydrogel to form the alphabet “SCU” (Fig. 3 f).

The rheological properties of the hydrogel were subsequently tested. For the SOD-AO hydrogel, G′ (storage moduli) increased from 9,000 to 11,000 Pa, and G″ (loss moduli) increased from 900 to 1,500 Pa with a FREQ sweep from 0.1 to 100 rad s−1 (Fig. 3 g). Such discrepancy between G′ and G″ showed hydrogel formation. When a higher strain (1,000 %) was applied, the hydrogel network collapsed, and the hydrogel became a liquid-like state (G″ > G′) (Fig. 3 h, Figure S5). The fabrication of the SOD mimicking centers surprisingly increased the mechanical properties of the hydrogel. As presented in Fig. 3 g–i and Figure S5, the SOD-AO and SOD hydrogel significantly exhibited higher modulus (9400 Pa) than the control and AO hydrogel (750 Pa). The rheologic tests of the hydrogel showed that the hydrogel could be fully disrupted into a liquid-like state under a 1,000 % strain (G″ > G′). When the applied strain was reduced to 1 %, the G″ and G′ immediately recovered to the original levels, indicating the excellent self-healing property of the hydrogel (Fig. 3 i), as reported previously.[21] Such self-healing capability should be subscribed to the dynamic bonds cross-linking the hydrogel.[22] The homogeneous structures, injectability, and self-healing ability increased the hydrogel’s clinical practicability and allowed it to sufficiently cover an irregularly shaped wound. Furthermore, the introduction of CQDs enabled the detection of the hydrogel via excited green fluorescence, thereby indicating the change in wound dressing (Figure S6), as previously reported.[23].

Tissue adhesion is essential for wound dressings. The lap shear test was used to test the adhesive strength of the hydrogel to porcine skin. As shown in Fig. S7a and b, the adhesion strength of the SOD-AO hydrogel reached 5.7 ± 1.5 kPa. Moreover, the hydrogel could tightly adhere to the surface of rat organs (including the skin, heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) (Fig. S7c–j). Moreover, the detachment of the hydrogel caused negligible influence on the surface of these organs (Fig. S7k).

The photothermal anti-infection ability was originally designed by introducing CQDs into the hydrogel. As presented in Fig. 4
a and b and Figure S8, irradiation of IR at 780 nm allowed the SOD-AO hydrogel to increase the local temperature from 25 °C to 72 °C within 30 s in vitro. This result indicated that this hydrogel could increase the local temperature faster than previous products.[24] In vivo tests revealed that the local temperature increased to 58 °C within 12 min (Fig. 4 c, d). The gentler temperature increase should be attributed to the thermotaxis in vivo. Moreover, Cu2+ coordination exerts a synergistic photothermal effect.[25] This excellent photothermal effect could possibly enhance microbial killing efficiency.[19].

A superoxide anion scavenging test using a commercially available kit was employed to evaluate the ROS scavenging ability of the hydrogel. The SOD, AO, and SOD-AO hydrogel could significantly inhibit the accumulation of superoxide anions produced by the xanthine/xanthine oxidase system (Fig. 4 e). Similar results were presented by ESR signals corresponding to superoxide free radicals (Fig. 4 f). These results showed that SOD mimicking centers and ZIF-8@Ts NPs in the hydrogel could independently scavenge superoxide anions. Such a superoxide anion scavenging ability would be improved through the high swelling ratio of the hydrogel (Fig. 4 g and Figure S9), which enabled the hydrogel to absorb a high volume of inflammatory exudation containing ROS.

Intelligent infection management behaviors of the hydrogel were obtained via the pH-responsive Schiff base and coordination bonds. The pH = 5.0 microenvironment induced rapid degradation of the SOD-AO hydrogel (Fig. 4 h and Figure S10), responsively releasing CQDs to intelligently impede microbial growth. The cumulative release of CQDs was measured by detecting the emitted fluorescence intensity at 515 nm. Upon exposure to an environment with pH 5.0, approximately 70 % of the CQDs were released into the environment within 12 h (Fig. 4 i). As expected, upon exposure to an environment with pH 5.0, the hydrogel and ZIF-8@Ts NPs inside it were disintegrated, and 80 % of Ts was rapidly released for intelligent antioxidation within 24 h (Fig. 4 j). These smart behaviors endowed the hydrogel with intelligent anti-infection and antioxidation capacities in response to infection exacerbation.

Owing to the fact that hemostasis was the main issue in wound healing. The SOD-AO and control hydrogel exhibited excellent hemostatic ability compared with the commercial hemostatic product (Hydrosorb® Gel). As presented in Fig. 5
a and S11a, the BCI was significantly lower in the SOD-AO and control hydrogel groups compared to that in the control and commercial hemostatic hydrogel groups. SEM revealed better red blood cell (RBC) surface adhesion and morphology in both the control hydrogel and SOD-AO hydrogel (Fig. 5 b). A large number of blood cells formed irregular aggregates on the surface of both the control hydrogel and SOD-AO hydrogel, whereas few RBC masses were observed on the surface of Hydrosorb® Gel. In a rat liver bleeding model (Fig. 5 c), the control hydrogel and SOD-AO hydrogel exhibited significantly low levels of blood loss (Fig. 5 d and Figure S11b) and faster hemostasis (Fig. 5 e and Figure S11c) compared with Hydrosorb® Gel. The high hemostasis competence of the SOD-AO hydrogel could be attributed to the positively charged CS-His and high swelling ratio of the hydrogel, which functioned in combination to entrap negatively charged RBCs in the blood.[26].

The microbial killing ability of the SOD-AO hydrogel was tested via in vitro bactericidal tests. The SOD-AO hydrogel combining PTT showed the highest antimicrobial efficiency. As presented in Fig. 6
a–c, few microbial clones existed in the SOD-AO hydrogel combining PTT group. This great microbial eradication ability was verified by bacteria live/dead staining (Fig. 6 d), which indicated that almost all of the bacteria in the SOD-AO hydrogel combining PTT group were dead. The SOD-AO hydrogel free of PTT also exhibited effective bactericidal ability, followed by the SOD hydrogel. Collectively, these data confirmed that the SOD mimicking centers could effectively kill the microbes. Such a strong bactericidal ability was further enhanced by PTT, endowing the hydrogel with higher bactericidal efficiency than hydrogel depending on antibiotics. [3,14].

The biocompatibility of the SOD-AO hydrogel was extensively validated. Basically, the hemolytic analysis revealed that all of the hydrogels exhibited slight hemolysis (<5%), as presented in Fig. 7 a, b. For the critical role of fibroblasts in wound healing,[27] L929 cells, a mouse fibroblast cell line, were used to test the hydrogel biocompatibility. Live/dead staining revealed that the cells co-cultured with hydrogel represented the same proliferation tendency as the cells cultured in a normal complete medium (Fig. 7 c). This viability tendency was further verified via the CCK-8 assay (Fig. 7 d). Taken together, the hydrogel exhibited comparable biocompatibility to previously reported wound dressings. [3,28,29].

Then, L929 cells were treated with lipopolysaccharide (LPS, 10 µg/mL) to mimic the infectious conditions of the wound before analyzing the scratch-healing ability of the hydrogel. The results indicated that LPS infection significantly impaired the normal proliferation and migration abilities of L929 cells. The inhibitory effect of L929 cell proliferation and migration could be completely rescued by the SOD-AO hydrogel and largely eliminated by the SOD or AO hydrogel (Fig. 7 e–g).

The ROS scavenging ability of the hydrogel was evaluated using RAW264.7 prestimulated with LPS (10 µg/mL). The high ROS level induced by LPS stimulation was downregulated to a normal level comparable to that of unstimulated macrophages through treatment with the SOD-AO hydrogel (Fig. 8
a). Furthermore, the SOD and AO hydrogel independently exhibited strong ROS scavenging capacity. These results illustrated the credible ROS scavenging capacities of the SOD mimicking centers and ZIF-8@Ts NPs in the fabricated hydrogel. The high ROS level was possibly relieved in two ways as follows: First, the SOD mimicking centers in the hydrogel catalyzed the decomposition of ROS released into the environment, subsequently attenuating mitochondrial oxygen stress.[30] Second, the released Ts directly reduced generated ROS and inhibited ROS generation.[31] Compared to hydrogel depending on antioxidative drugs alone, the multiple mechanisms increased the potency of the hydrogel in scavenging ROS.[32].

The ROS scavenging ability enabled the SOD-AO hydrogel to transform M1 phenotype macrophages into the M2 phenotype. This was verified by analyzing the expression of specific markers for M1 (CD86) and M2 (CD206) in RAW264.7 cells. As presented in Fig. 8 b, upon LPS stimulation, 69.4 % of the cells were positive for CD86, and only 38.1 % of the cells were positive for CD206, whereas the frequency of Pan-macrophage (CD68 + ) cells was not significantly different among the groups (Figure S12). Treatment with SOD, AO and SOD-AO hydrogel could significantly upregulate the frequency of CD206 + macrophages and downregulate the frequency of CD86 + macrophages. The SOD-AO hydrogel exhibited the strongest M2 transformation efficiency; the frequency of CD86 + cells was downregulated to 18.2 %, and that of CD206 + cells was upregulated to 63.3 %. These results indicated that the SOD mimicking centers and ZIF-8@Ts NPs could independently convert polarized M1 phenotype macrophages to M2 phenotype. Therefore, a beneficial M2 transformation balance could be maintained through the sequential work of SOD mimicking centers and ZIF-8@Ts NPs, even in a reinfected wound.

Macroscopic wound closure analysis revealed that SOD-AO hydrogel combining PTT enabled a faster wound closure than the other groups ( Fig. 9
a, b), with ∼ 90 % wound closure achieved by day 7 and complete wound healing by day 10. On day 14, exuberant hair was observed on the newborn skin in the SOD-AO hydrogel combining PTT group, indicating the regeneration of hair follicles in the newly regenerated skin. The SOD-AO hydrogel group exhibited a relatively lower wound closure rate but still higher than those of the other groups, with ∼ 80 % wound closure achieved by day 7 and nearly complete wound healing by day 10. Even after 14 days, the wound in the blank control and control hydrogel groups failed to heal completely. The excellent healing efficiency should be subscribed to the multifunction of the hydrogel (Figure S13). [14,29] Similar to the in vitro antimicrobial results, the SOD-AO hydrogel combining PTT exhibited robust anti-infection capacity and almost no bacterial colony was observed on day 5 (Fig. 9 c, d).

Histological analysis was conducted to evaluate the details of the newborn tissue in the wound. Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) staining demonstrated in situ regeneration of epithelial cells, deposition of plenty of collagens, a mass of fibroblasts, and indistinct wound boundary in the SOD-AO hydrogel combining PTT group (Fig. 10
a, j). Moreover, dense de novo hair follicle formation, high neovascularization, and gland regeneration were observed, indicating functional skin regeneration. By contrast, the wound in the blank control group was filled with scars and even abscesses.

Collagen fibers, working in wound contraction and basal layer reconstruction, were detected in the new skin through Masson staining (Fig. 10 b, k). The results indicated that SOD-AO hydrogel combining PTT facilitated collagen deposition (∼90 %) in the newly generated tissue compared with the other groups. By contrast, the blank control group exhibited poor collagen deposition. COL-I and COL-III (Fig. 10 c, d), the main types of collagens in the skin, were further specifically distinguished via immunohistochemistry (IHC) staining, which demonstrated a similar tendency as the Masson staining. Also, a significantly higher degree of neovascularization was observed in the SOD-AO hydrogel combining PTT group (Fig. 10 a, l).

The mechanism of promoting in situ skin regeneration was further investigated via immunofluorescence. Compared with the other groups, we found a significantly higher frequency of cells in the regenerated tissue positive for CD206 and negative for iNOS in the SOD-AO hydrogel combining PTT group, in contrast to the blank control group (Fig. 10 e, f). This indicated that M2 transformation was enabled in this group. The M2 macrophages exerted an anti-inflammatory effect, which was indicated by the significantly higher frequency of IL-10 positive cells and lower frequency of TNF-α positive cells in the SOD-AO hydrogel combining PTT group (Fig. 10 g, h) compared with the control group. Such an anti-inflammatory effect can promote tissue proliferation.[19] Moreover, M2 phenotype macrophages cooperate with Zn2+ to modulate tissue remodeling on the one hand and cooperate with Cu2+ to facilitate vascularization in the newly regenerated tissue on the other hand.[33] As presented in Fig. 10 i, CD 31 + endothelial cells appear in a significantly larger population in the SOD-AO hydrogel combining PTT group than in the other groups. Collectively, the SOD-AO hydrogel can promote in situ skin regeneration by modulating the transformation of M2 macrophages, which facilitates tissue proliferation, vascularization, and remodeling. Moreover, these results further indicated that the SOD-AO hydrogel can protect the wound from reinfection-induced healing cessation, probably by maintaining the M1–M2 polarization balance with its responsive release behaviors.

In the present study, an injectable and self-healing hydrogel with photothermal anti-infection, SOD mimicking, and intelligent infection management behaviors was developed for refractory wound healing. The SOD mimicking centers with photothermal effect were successfully synthesized by coordinating CS-His and imidazole-functionalized photothermal CQDs with Cu2+. The hydrogel was further endowed with intelligent infection management behaviors by dynamically cross-linking CS-His with OHA and loading ZIF-8@Ts in the hydrogel. Such a hydrogel could effectively cover the wound sites and exhibited excellent hemostatic ability both in vitro and in vivo. With the assistance of PTT, the hydrogel could eliminate early infection. In response to the infection-induced low-pH microenvironment, the hydrogel rapidly released CQDs for anti-infection and Ts for ROS scavenging, thereby providing comprehensively intelligent management of wound infection. By effectively scavenging ROS, the prepared hydrogel promoted the M2 transformation of macrophages and promoted the inhibitory effect of infection on fibroblast proliferation and migration. In vivo results verified that the hydrogel combined with PTT could promote angiogenesis and collagen deposition by modulating the inflammatory responses in the wound. These favorable properties indicated that the SOD-AO hydrogel had considerable potential to induce in situ regeneration of functional skin in refractory wounds.

Chuan Guo: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Software, Writing – original draft. Yu Wang: Investigation, Methodology, Software, Visualization. Hui Liu: Investigation, Methodology, Software, Visualization. Ye Wu: Investigation, Methodology. Yi Wang: Investigation, Methodology. Zhenxing Cao: Methodology, Software. Weilong Li: Investigation, Methodology. Yan Peng: Investigation, Methodology. Hui Xiong: Investigation, Methodology. Biqiang Jin: Methodology, Software. Qingquan Kong: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Jinrong Wu: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing.
